title: Typed inert-container registries — one parameterized registry vs sibling Custom*Type registries over <!-- -->/<template type>/<script type>, and the native extension points each rides
summary: Prep research for decision #1986 (mechanism-for the resolved #1983 directive-form ruling; we:reports/2026-06-30-typed-inert-container-registries.md). #1983 ratified the directive FORM — typed `<template type="kind">` (inert) / `<!-- ns:name -->` (live), `is=`-free — and carved the REGISTRATION MECHANISM to #1986. The question here is the registry that implements `type=`: (1) which registry hosts the ratified `type=` form, (2) one parameterized `CustomTypeRegistry` vs three concrete sibling registries (`CustomCommentRegistry` / `CustomTemplateType` / `CustomScriptType`) on the existing `HTMLRegistry` base, and (3) whether a `<script type>` sibling earns its place yet. CODE GROUND TRUTH (frontierui): two registries already exist as concrete siblings on one base — `CustomCommentRegistry` and `CustomAttributeRegistry` both extend `HTMLRegistry` (fui:plugs/core/HTMLRegistry.ts:21), which already carries the shared define/get/name-map shape; each sibling adds only its own upgrade(root) walk. So a `CustomTypeRegistry` 'shared shape' ALREADY EXISTS as the base class. The `observedAttributes`/`attributeChangedCallback` capability is NOT `is=`-only: `CustomAttributeRegistry` already provides it for non-element hosts via a MutationObserver it owns (fui:plugs/webbehaviors/CustomAttributeRegistry.ts:84,99,207,425-427,526-576), and `portal` (the only `is=` directive, fui:plugs/webportals/PortalDirective.ts:123) gets attribute reactivity natively today — so migrating portal onto a `type=` registry loses no capability. FINDING F1 — `<script type>` is a native OPEN kind-space: the HTML spec defines unrecognized `type` values as 'data blocks… not processed by the user agent, but instead by author script or other tools' (html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/scripting.html) — `CustomScriptType` rides a genuine native extension point; caveat: the spec recommends a non-JS-MIME string, so a bare `type="injector"` is mildly off-guidance (`application/we-injector` is a #1987 question). F2 — but native gives INERTNESS, not a registry: `HTMLScriptElement.supports()` is a CLOSED set (classic/module/importmap/speculationrules); the registry + define/upgrade/whenDefined lifecycle is a WE invention over a native inert substrate. F3 — `<template type>` has NO native precedent: every standardized `<template>` attribute is a shadow-root MODIFIER (`shadowrootmode` et al.), and `shadowrootmode` is explicitly a MODE (adjectival), not an is-a kind (MDN template ref); `type` is undefined on `<template>`, so a WE convention is collision-safe. F4 — `type=`-as-is-a-kind is the platform's pervasive discriminator idiom (`<input type>`, `<button type>`, `<ol type>`, `<script type>`), contrasted against adjectival `…mode` — so `<template type="if">` ('this template IS an if-block') is in the grain of native `type=` semantics (partly UNCERTAIN — inferred from the cross-element pattern + the shadowrootmode contrast, no single 'type means is-a' citation). F5 — `is=` customized built-ins are VENDOR-REJECTED: WebKit's standards-position is 'oppose' (github.com/WebKit/standards-positions/issues/97); the platform's replacement is `ElementInternals.type` (github.com/whatwg/html/issues/11061), i.e. discriminate-on-the-element — so `type=` over `is=` follows where standards went. F6 — the native template-instantiation roadmap (Template Instantiation → DOM Parts; w3.org/2025/03/26-webcomponents-minutes) anticipates 'looping, conditionals, plus customization' + a processor seam, so `<template type="if">` is a forward-compatible bet — though the native proposals lean toward a JS-passed processor not a `type` ATTRIBUTE, so the attribute-keyed part is a WE invention (UNCERTAIN). F7 — a runtime registry keyed by an element discriminator is the DOMINANT framework model: Vue `app.directive()`, Angular, Alpine register/match at runtime by a discriminator on the element (vuejs.org/guide/reusability/custom-directives); Svelte is compile-time, Lit a per-call factory — WE's `type`-keyed registry mirrors Vue/Angular/Alpine. F8 — lifecycle parity target = `CustomElementRegistry`: define/upgrade/whenDefined + observedAttributes/attributeChangedCallback (MDN); `upgrade` matters because inert containers, like un-upgraded custom elements, can exist in the DOM before the registry loads. SYNTHESIS for #1986: the `type=` form must register through a VALUE-space registry — `CustomAttribute` dispatches on attribute NAME tree-wide and cannot express a `type`-value discriminator without colliding with `<input type>`/`<script type>`, so the new registry is a FORCED consequence of #1983 (not a fresh weigh); three concrete siblings on `HTMLRegistry` beat one parameterized god-object (which would re-implement the base) under separate-and-decouple + the two shipped siblings; `CustomScriptType` is BUILD-NOW (the prep skeptic flipped an initial NOT-YET): `<script type="injector">` already ships a bespoke idempotent boot-scan, `applyDeclarativeInjectors` at fui:plugs/webinjectors/declarativeInjector.ts:107-147, which IS an upgrade(root) walk — so the registry absorbs it (no over-build) and deletes the duplicate scanner; 'no consumer yet' is a banned defer reason once the contract is codified (we:docs/agent/backlog-workflow.md:521) and the once-named defer trigger is counterfactual (#1968 resolved zero-node, an event front door, not a script annotation). Codifies into we:docs/agent/block-standard.md#directive-form (the slot #1983 left at :397), composing with the Custom[Name]Registry naming convention (platform-decisions.md:618-627) and registry name-validation scope (:654-680).
